acceptedAnswer.text: ATEX refers to two European directives for controlling explosive atmospheres: Directive 2014/34/EU covers equipment and protective systems, while Directive 1999/92/EC covers workplace safety. ATEX derives from the French 'Atmosphères Explosibles'. Any electrical or mechanical equipment used in classified hazardous areas within the EU must be ATEX certified.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between ATEX and IECEx?

acceptedAnswer.text: ATEX is a European Union regulatory requirement (mandatory in the EU). IECEx is an international voluntary certification scheme managed by the IEC. Equipment sold in the EU needs ATEX certification. IECEx certificates are accepted in over 35 countries and can speed up national approvals, but they don't replace ATEX within the EU.

mainEntity.name: What are Zone 0, Zone 1, and Zone 2?

acceptedAnswer.text: These are gas hazard classifications. Zone 0: explosive atmosphere is present continuously or for long periods. Zone 1: explosive atmosphere is likely during normal operation. Zone 2: explosive atmosphere is not likely during normal operation, but may occur briefly. The higher the zone number, the lower the risk. Equipment for Zone 0 must have the highest protection level (EPL Ga).

mainEntity.name: What do the letters in Ex markings mean?

acceptedAnswer.text: An Ex marking like 'Ex db IIC T6 Gb' breaks down as: 'Ex' = explosion protected, 'db' = flameproof enclosure (protection level b), 'IIC' = gas group (hydrogen/acetylene), 'T6' = temperature class (max 85°C surface), 'Gb' = Equipment Protection Level (suitable for Zone 1). Each part specifies what the equipment is certified for.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between Ex d and Ex e?

acceptedAnswer.text: Ex d (flameproof) contains an explosion inside a robust enclosure — the gap geometry cools escaping gases below ignition temperature. Ex e (increased safety) prevents ignition from occurring in the first place by eliminating sparks, hot surfaces, and arcs through enhanced design margins. Ex d is used for switchgear and motors; Ex e for junction boxes, terminals, and lighting.

Intrinsic safety limits the electrical energy in a circuit so it cannot produce a spark or thermal effect capable of igniting an explosive atmosphere — even under fault conditions. Ex ia (highest level) remains safe with two simultaneous faults and is suitable for Zone 0. Ex ib allows one fault (Zone 1). It is the only protection concept that is inherently safe rather than containing an explosion.

mainEntity.name: What gas groups exist and what do they mean?

acceptedAnswer.text: Group I is for mining (methane). Group II is for surface industries: IIA (propane — least stringent), IIB (ethylene), IIC (hydrogen/acetylene — most stringent). Group III is for dust: IIIA (flyings), IIIB (non-conductive dust), IIIC (conductive dust — most stringent). Equipment certified for a higher group (e.g., IIC) can be used in lower groups (IIA, IIB).

mainEntity.name: What are temperature classes T1 through T6?

acceptedAnswer.text: Temperature classes define the maximum surface temperature of equipment: T1 (450°C), T2 (300°C), T3 (200°C), T4 (135°C), T5 (100°C), T6 (85°C). The equipment surface temperature must stay below the auto-ignition temperature of the gas present. T6 is the most restrictive. Most industrial gases are covered by T3 or T4.

mainEntity.name: How long does ATEX certification take?

acceptedAnswer.text: Typical timelines: 3-6 months for straightforward products with established protection methods. 6-12 months for complex products or novel designs. 12-18+ months if design modifications are needed after testing. The process includes: application, design review, prototype testing, test report (ExTR), quality system audit, and certificate issuance.

mainEntity.name: What is an EPL (Equipment Protection Level)?

acceptedAnswer.text: EPL defines the reliability of protection. For gas: Ga (very high, Zone 0), Gb (high, Zone 1), Gc (enhanced, Zone 2). For dust: Da (Zone 20), Db (Zone 21), Dc (Zone 22). For mining: Ma (remains energized), Mb (de-energized in emergency). EPL bridges zone classification and equipment selection — it tells you which zones the equipment can be installed in.

mainEntity.name: Can I use Zone 1 equipment in Zone 2?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Equipment certified for a more hazardous zone can always be used in a less hazardous zone. Zone 0 equipment works in Zones 0, 1, and 2. Zone 1 equipment works in Zones 1 and 2. This applies equally to dust zones (20, 21, 22). The reverse is never allowed — Zone 2 equipment cannot be installed in Zone 1.

A Notified Body is an organization designated by an EU member state to assess products against ATEX directive requirements. They perform EU Type Examination (Module B), issue certificates, and audit manufacturer quality systems. Examples include DEKRA, PTB, BASEEFA, INERIS, and LCIE. For IECEx, the equivalent is an Examination Body (ExCB).
